summary: A step-by-step tutorial demonstrating how to build a distributed whiteboard application using Firestr, a peer-to-peer communication platform. The post walks through creating a drawing canvas with color selection in Lua, then adds networking capabilities so multiple users can draw together in real-time by sending line coordinates as messages.
key_points: Firestr uses Lua scripting with a custom widget API to build peer-to-peer applications
The whiteboard app supports multi-color drawing with a simple canvas and button interface
Network functionality is added by serializing draw events into messages with key-value pairs
The distributed drawing synchronization requires only about 20 lines of networking code
Testing P2P apps locally is possible by running a second Firestr instance with a different home directory and port
money_quotes: I have just added a widget to draw lines, so of course the natural thing to do is create a drawing program.
Let's make it more fun by making it work where you can draw together with a friend.
I need to add more wiz bang. Oh, and I need help with security!
key_insight: Building a real-time distributed drawing application can be surprisingly simple when the underlying peer-to-peer platform handles the networking complexity, requiring only basic message serialization to synchronize state between users.
